page_title: Socioeconomic Stratification of Urban Nature in England

sections.heading: Green Walls of Privilege: How Tree Cover Tracks Deprivation Across England
sections.subtitle: An airborne, building-level assessment of the 3-30-300 greening rule across England — and who it leaves behind.

sections.heading: A simple rule for green cities
sections.quote: Every resident should see at least 3 trees from home, live in a neighbourhood with 30% canopy cover, and be within 300 metres of a public park.
sections.attribution: The 3-30-300 rule — adopted from Singapore to New York, yet never tested at national scale

sections.heading: The same country looks green or grey — depending on the metric
sections.body: Cities still lack standardised, high-resolution ways to measure who actually benefits from green infrastructure. The stakes are concrete: view England's trees per person and the rural North looks greenest; switch to trees per km² and the dense, affluent South East pulls ahead, with many areas exceeding 3,000 trees per km². The metric you pick changes where greening looks most needed.
sections.insight: Per capita and absolute density invert the map — a contradiction that coarse, inconsistent indicators routinely hide.
sections.figure_alt: Choropleth maps of England showing trees per person versus trees per square kilometre, which produce opposite patterns.

sections.heading: One framework, national scale
sections.body: We built the first open-source, national, building-level assessment of the 3-30-300 rule — pairing open LiDAR with high-performance computing (Apache Sedona and Google Earth Engine) to measure tree proximity, canopy availability and park accessibility for every building in England.

sections.heading: Trees yes, canopy and parks no
sections.body: Only 0.1% of urban neighbourhoods satisfy the full 3-30-300 rule. The '3' is the easy win — in London almost 40% of 8.2 million residents live within 25 m of at least three trees. But the 30% canopy target is the great national deficit, met by only a minority of people in every region, and park access trails the tree rule. Put all three together and the system nearly collapses.
sections.figure_alt: Bar chart of the population in each English region meeting each component of the 3-30-300 rule.

sections.heading: A green gap that tracks wealth
sections.body: Break each metric down by deprivation decile and a systematic pattern appears. In every region, from the North West to London, the least-deprived neighbourhoods have more trees nearby and higher canopy cover than the most-deprived. Ambient greenness — the street trees and garden canopy that surround a home — is consistently skewed toward wealthier communities.
sections.insight: Canopy cover and tree proximity rise with affluence in every single region of England.
sections.figure_alt: Boxplots of tree count, canopy cover, park distance and water distance across deprivation deciles, grouped by region.

sections.heading: Parks flip the script — and leafy regions hide the sharpest divides
sections.body: Park access runs the other way: the most-deprived, densest urban cores are often closer to a public park than affluent suburbs, a reverse green gap. Yet the greenest places conceal the worst inequality — a Gini analysis flags Inner London, Birmingham and Manchester as hotspots of unequal access to both nearby trees and parks.
sections.figure_alt: Maps of average canopy cover and a bivariate Gini map of environmental inequality across England.

sections.heading: Green access is a new axis of inequality
sections.body: Treating proximity, canopy and park access as distinct dimensions reveals exposure profiles that a single greenness index like NDVI obscures — and that vary systematically with deprivation and urban form. Distance to a park says nothing about its quality, safety or usability; a canopy percentage ignores what those trees actually do. The real power of the 3-30-300 rule is diagnostic.
sections.cta: Policy must measure not just how much nature a city has, but who can actually use it.

sections.heading: Credits
sections.authors: Andrés Camilo Zúñiga-González, Anil Madhavapeddy, Ronita Bardhan
